May 17 2019

<div class="blog-header">

# Play Stupid Games, Win Stupid Prizes

<div class="blog-subtitle">

Competition will blind you to greater games

</div>

<div class="podcast-container style-1">

<div class="audio-player-container">

<div class="controls">

<a href="#" class="not-smooth play-pause-btn"></a>

<div class="dropdown">

May 17 2019

</div>

</div>

</div>

<div class="content">

**Nivi:**<span style="font-weight: 400"> When you’re being authentic,
you don’t mind competition that much. It pisses you off and inspires
some fear, jealousy and other emotions. But you don’t really mind
because you’re oriented towards the goal and the mission. Worst-case,
you might get some ideas from them. And often there are ways to work
with the competition in a positive way that ends up increasing the size
of the market for you. </span>

**Naval:**<span style="font-weight: 400"> It depends on the nature of
the business. The best Silicon Valley tech industry businesses tend to
be winner-take-all. When you see competition, it can make you fly into a
rage</span><span style="font-weight: 400">.</span><span style="font-weight: 400">
Because it really does endanger everything you’ve built.</span>

<span id="more-28492610"></span>

<span style="">If I’m opening a restaurant and a more interesting
version of the same restaurant opens in a different town, that’s
fantastic. I’m going to copy what’s working and drop what’s not working.
So it depends on the nature of the business. </span>

Often, <span style="">businesses that seem to be in direct competition
really aren’t. They end up adjacent or slightly different. You’re one
step away from a completely different business, and sometimes you need
to take that step. You’re not going to take it if you’re busy fighting
over a booby prize. </span>

<span style="">You’re playing a stupid game. You’re going to win a
stupid prize. </span>It’s not obvious right now because you’re blinded
by competition. But three years from now, it’ll be obvious.

**My first company got caught in the wrong game**

One <span style="">of my first startups was
[Epinions](https://www.google.com/search?q=epinions&rlz=1C1CHBF_enUS795US795&oq=Epinions&aqs=chrome.0.0l6.216j0j9&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8),
an online product review site that was independent of Amazon. That space
eventually turned into TripAdvisor and Yelp, which is where we should
have gone. </span>

We should have done more local reviews. A review of a scarce item like a
local restaurant is more valuable than one of an item like a camera that
has 1,000 reviews on Amazon.

Before we could get there, we got caught up in the comparison-shopping
game. We merged with DealTime and competed with a bunch of
price-comparison engines—mySimon, PriceGrabber, NexTag and Bizrate,
which became Shopzilla. We were caught in fierce competition with each
other.

<span style="">That whole space went to zero because Amazon won e-tail
completely. There was no need for price comparison. Everyone just went
to Amazon. </span>

<span style="">We got the booby prize because we were caught up in
competition with a bunch of our peers. We should have been looking at
what the consumer really wanted and being authentic to ourselves, which
was reviews, not price comparison. We should have gone further into
esoteric items where customers had less data and wanted reviews more
badly. </span>

<span style="">If we stayed authentic to ourselves, we would have done
better. </span>
